entry.title: Re: Issue in DMEEX – Unable to Update Payment Structure D...
entry.content:
Hi,

In general, to the Synchronization in DMEEX Topic:
Once you open a child tree which has its parent tree modified and you trigger a change on a child tree, then pressing the pop-up option “Yes” will only trigger the Synchronization process. It does not mean the Synchronization will happen straight away. Meaning that the user will be taken to the synchronization part and will have the change to review all changes first. Only then either accept or decline the synchronization and with that the changes.

Please also refer to SAP Help

Synchronizing Format Trees:
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/3cb1182b4a184bdd93f8d62e3f1f0741/985a215156914ab6848ee4be3c36862a.html?version=LATEST&ai=true&locale=en-US

and for his particular case it is the Synchronization from Child Tree:
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/d93424d8ff23402f80ac55a4cad80270/80ae81942b8b4c709bee0992ba31a1bb.html?version=LATEST&ai=true&locale=en-US

3379435 - DMEEX - How to delete a node in a child tree ? 
3099764 - Node settings are not editable in transaction code DMEEX 

Summary:
If you do not synchronize, you can still edit the tree.
And if you want to synchronize, you will have the change to review the changes and if you don’t like it, they can cancel the synchronization.

Best regards,
Mónika

entry.title: Set FO document currency during creation using TransportationOrderGenericRequest_in
entry.content:
Hello,

we're using web service TransportationOrderGenericRequest_in to create freight orders (FOs) based on freight units (FUs). The document currency of the FO is derived from the purchasing organizational view of the carrier, maintained on business partner level.

For specific processes, however, we'd need a deviating/different document currency than the one maintained in master data.

Is there a way to set the document currency using the web service? Either during creation or with a change call?

In class /SCMTMS/CL_TOR_GENRC_RQ_IN_SRV, method MAP_TRANSPORTATIONORDER, there's an ABAP comment that "DefaultCurrencyCode" is "currently not mapped in inbound".

Thanks & regards

Wolfgang

entry.title: SAP Signavio Process Modeler: What’s new in the July Release
entry.content:
Process modeling has never been a one-size-fits-all activity. Some teams start with a whiteboard sketch, others with a spreadsheet, and many with a rough text description handed off to a BPMN expert. The challenge still has always been the same: how do you turn these varied inputs into standardized, high-quality process models defining your operating model without losing time to manual rework?

Building on its proven ability to generate BPMN diagrams from text descriptions, the July 2026 SAP Signavio release introduces three powerful enhancements to the AI-assisted process modeler. Together, these features reduce manual effort, lower the barrier for non-expert modelers, and accelerate the journey from idea to standardized BPMN model. Beyond text descriptions, users can now transform images and tables into process diagrams and refine AI-generated models through natural language. These innovations bring process modeling within reach of every stakeholder, not just BPMN experts.

Let’s dive into what’s new:

AI-assisted process modeling

  • Image to process

Turn process sketches into BPMN models with AI

Process discovery often begins visually. Teams sketch ideas on whiteboards, run brainstorming sessions in Miro or Mural, or maintain legacy diagrams in Visio, Lucidchart, or Draw.io. But translating those visual artifacts into standardized BPMN diagrams has traditionally required significant manual effort, especially when third-party tools don’t offer clean, standards-compliant exports.

With the general availability of the image to process feature, users can now effortlessly transform visual process diagrams into BPMN process models. Thanks to flexible image input handling, the feature accepts images and screenshots from third-party tools like Miro, Mural, Visio, Lucidchart, or Draw.io, as well as hand-drawn sketches, which is especially valuable for tools that don’t offer standards-compliant exports. Through intelligent image-to-process conversion, the AI automatically extracts process steps, IT systems, and lanes from the visual content, and is designed to generate accurate and contextually relevant BPMN diagrams.

The result is a drastic reduction in the manual effort spent recreating processes and onboarding content into your process repository. Teams can focus their energy on process refinement and innovation, rather than redrawing shapes and connectors. Visual brainstorming sessions no longer get lost in translation, and the transition from informal concepts to formal process models becomes seamless, enhancing collaboration between business stakeholders and BPMN experts.

  • Table to process

Transform spreadsheets into BPMN process models

Many organizations already document processes in structured tabular formats. Spreadsheets, requirement templates, and process inventories are everywhere. These formats are excellent for capturing detail and standardizing documentation but converting them into BPMN diagrams has historically required significant manual effort.

With table to process, modelers can now transform tabular process descriptions directly into BPMN diagrams. Users who don’t have tabular process descriptions already can download the best practice tabular template as well. It provides a guided, structured format for capturing process details, designed for optimal compatibility with our AI-assisted process modeler. Once completed, the file can be imported directly into the tool, where AI-powered diagram generation analyzes the structured data to automatically produce a process diagram that’s more compliant with BPMN 2.0.

This capability speeds up time to value by rapidly generating process models from existing tabular documentation, while also increasing consistency in how processes are captured across the organization. The template becomes a powerful collaboration tool for gathering requirements from business stakeholders, and provides a structured alternative to free-form text, giving teams another reliable path from documentation to model. Beyond efficiency, it also helps users understand which information is truly relevant for process documentation, decreasing reliance on ad-hoc templates, and improving the comprehensiveness and quality of process knowledge.

  • Modify an AI-generated diagram

Refine process models through natural language

Generating the first draft of a BPMN diagram from a text description is a powerful starting point, but every modeler knows that first drafts rarely capture the full picture. Reviewing and adjusting the model to ensure it accurately reflects the real process is a critical step, and until now, that often meant reverting to manual editing or restarting the modeling process entirely.

This new capability enables process modelers to iterate on AI-generated diagrams using natural language. By typing follow up instructions, users can simply modify the AI-generated model, such as adjusting activities, adding or removing gateways, restructuring flows, or clarifying semantics, and AI applies those changes while preserving the diagram’s existing structure and context. Refinement becomes a conversation, not a rework exercise.

 

By lowering the barrier for non-expert modelers, this capability makes process modeling accessible to a broader audience. Business stakeholders can refine models simply by describing what they want, while experienced modelers save time by avoiding manual edits. Most importantly, users retain full control over progressive improvements, building trust in AI-generated models one iteration at a time.

Modernized UI for BPMN Simulation

A guided experience for process analysis

BPMN simulation enables teams to test process scenarios, validate design decisions, and identify potential bottlenecks before changes are implemented. With the July 2026 release, SAP Signavio introduces a redesigned BPMN simulation experience that makes these powerful capabilities easier and more intuitive to use, while continuing to leverage the same trusted simulation engine users already rely on. 

The new experience is aligned with the SAP Fiori design language, bringing a cleaner and more modern user interface that feels consistent with the broader SAP Signavio portfolio. While the underlying simulation logic remains unchanged, the user experience has been reimagined to help analysts move from configuration to insights more efficiently.

 A new step-by-step progress indicator guides users through the simulation journey, providing clear visibility into each stage, from configuration and execution to results analysis. This structured workflow reduces complexity and helps both new and occasional users navigate simulations with greater confidence.

To further streamline analysis, simulation results are now displayed in a dedicated side panel that remains visible alongside the process diagram. This side-by-side view allows analysts to evaluate outcomes while keeping the process context in sight, making it easier to understand performance impacts and identify improvement opportunities without constantly switching between screens.

We've also simplified simulation setup through inline parameter editing. Instead of opening separate modal windows, users can now adjust simulation parameters directly within the working environment, reducing unnecessary clicks and enabling faster experimentation with different scenarios.

Together, these enhancements deliver a more intuitive and efficient simulation experience, helping process analysts spend less time navigating the tool and more time generating actionable insights. Whether validating process redesigns or comparing alternative scenarios, users can now analyze process performance with greater speed, clarity, and ease.

Conclusion

The July 2026 release reinforces SAP Signavio’s commitment to making process modeling more intuitive, inclusive, and efficient across the enterprise. By enabling users to generate BPMN diagrams from images, refine AI-generated models through natural language, and transform structured tables into BPMN-aligned diagrams, this release removes the friction that has traditionally stood between process ideas and process execution.

Together, these capabilities reduce manual effort, accelerate time to value, and empower a broader range of contributors, from business stakeholders to seasoned BPMN experts, to participate meaningfully in process design. As organizations continue to navigate complex transformation initiatives, SAP Signavio provides the intelligent, AI-first foundation needed to model with confidence, collaborate with clarity, and drive continuous improvement at scale.

Want to know more about this release?

Check out the highlight blog post and explore what else we’ve prepared across the entire SAP Signavio Process Transformation Suite.
We look forward to seeing you on the SAP Signavio Community to share feedback, ask questions, and discuss with your peers. Feel free to drop your thoughts in the comments below, or catch our Release Webcast on Demand for a deeper look at what’s new.

entry.title: SAP SuccessFactors Variable Pay worksheet
entry.content: Hi, we are experiencing an issue in our SAP SuccessFactors Variable Pay worksheet: employees who have concurrent employment (a primary and a secondary assignment, each with its own User ID) appear as two separate rows in the worksheet, as expected, but both rows are completely blank - no Employee Status, Company, Job Title or Pay Grade is displayed, even though the corresponding data exists and is correctly maintained in Employee Central for both assignments. We have reproduced this with two different test employees, both showing the identical behavior. We have already verified the following: the Employee History data (Manage Data / Edit Employee History) is fully populated and correct for both User IDs of the affected employees, including Status, dates, Job Grade, Company, Salary and Target Amount; we have also completed a Basic Import of employee data for both User IDs through Import Employee Data; and we have relaunched/refreshed the affected worksheets after both imports, without any change in the blank display. Could you please advise on how concurrent employment should be correctly configured and mapped for Variable Pay, and help us identify why these fields remain blank despite the data being present in Employee Central. Thank you!

entry.title: Material Status and blocking of PR creation
entry.content:
Hi all,

I am currently trying to understand if this system behaviour is correct or if I do something wrong. I am currently trying to set up a small example for a customer in regards to Material discontinuation.

I have assembly Group X, in that there is material A, which is discontinued by material B somewhen next year. When a material is discontinued the customer also changes the material status in Basic Data 1 and MRP1 View. Customer now expects the system to not create a PR for that material, when that status is set.

I checked OMS4 and see that everything is marked with "A", so only a warning, meaning I can still run MRP for that material. Also when trying to convert to PR to PO i get a warning but system still lets me do it.

I changed it to a material status that has everything set to "B", error message. Now when I run the MRP for the assembly group X, still a PR gets created for the material B, but I cant convert it to PO.

 

Is this correct system behaviour or is there a way to completely block creation of PR for material B with a material status?

 

Thank you for your help.

entry.title: Crystal Reports Designer Performance Issue on Private Cloud Compared to Local Server
entry.content:
Hi SAP Expert Community,

I would appreciate your guidance regarding a Crystal Reports Designer performance issue.

Environment

I have two environments:

1. Local On-Premises Server

  • SAP Business One for Microsoft SQL Server
  • Crystal Reports Designer installed
  • Report opens almost instantly (within approximately 1 second).
  • Dragging, moving, and resizing report fields is immediate and very smooth.

2. Private Cloud Environment

  • SAP Business One for Microsoft SQL Server
  • Separate Server:
    • Microsoft SQL Server + SAP SLD
  • Separate Terminal/RDP Server:
    • SAP Business One Client
    • SAP Crystal Reports Designer
  • Both servers are configured with 12 vCPUs and 64 GB RAM, which is sufficient for our workload and follows SAP's recommended architecture.

Issue

On the Private Cloud environment:

  • Opening Crystal Reports Designer takes approximately 2–3 seconds.
  • Dragging or repositioning report fields has a noticeable delay of 1–2 seconds before the field moves.
  • Overall report designing is less responsive than the local server.

entry.content: Please contact SAP Support

entry.title: reCAP 2026 convinced me that we’re entering the AI-native era of SAP development
entry.content:
Prelude

Just like the previous reCAP conferences that I attended before, I arrived at reCAP 2026 in St. Leon-Rot with a simple goal: learn what’s next for the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP). Like many CAP developers, I expected sessions on new framework capabilities, tooling support, best practices, and roadmap updates. AI was certainly on my radar, but I viewed it as a helpful coding assistant—not something that would fundamentally reshape how I think about building enterprise applications.

By the end of the conference, that perspective had completely changed.

The Turning Point

The turning point came during the session on the CAP MCP development server. At first, I assumed it was simply another developer productivity feature. But as the session unfolded, I realized SAP wasn’t just integrating AI into CAP—it was redesigning the development experience around AI.

So what is a CAP MCP development Server? To put it simply, an MCP server – it stands for Model Context Protocol server. Because CAP ships with an MCP server out of the box, AI assistants can understand your project, access framework knowledge, and interact with development tools without custom integrations. This means that the MCP server is now an integral part of the CAP framework that will give a significant productivity boost when using AI.

reCAP 2026 session: AI-assisted development of CAP Apps

Another aspect that is evolving is the prevalence of agentic AI. Just when I thought that much of the AI agent support is pretty much handled by SAP’s Joule (pretty much a locked feature in S/4HANA), now with the help of AI + MCP servers for CAP/UI5, you can build your very own AI agent -- with this, SAP developers can have their cake and eat it too!

reCAP 2026 session: Building Code-Based AI Agents with CAP and UI5: From Concept to Production

There are other very interesting sessions in the conference, but I just highlighted here the sessions that served as a turning point that something significant has arrived in SAP development, and these sessions became the focus of this blog post.

AI is no longer a coding assistant. It is becoming part of the application architecture itself!

Why does this matter for every SAP Developer?

The role of SAP developers is evolving. Future SAP developers may spend less time writing CRUD logic, generating boilerplate, and wiring services. With the time saved, developers can spend more time designing domain models, defining business processes, validating AI outputs, orchestrating agents, and ensuring governance and security.

Read that again – “validating AI outputs”! AI is not here to replace developers, but instead, the skill set shifts upward. Let me rephrase that to stress my point – “AI is not here to replace skillful developers”. And this is why I said earlier that the role of SAP developers is evolving – it will change the way we work.

I’ve been in the SAP industry long enough to witness and experience the evolution of how developers build applications. Looking back, every major shift in SAP development has changed not only the tools we use, but also what we consider valuable developer skills. First is building applications using ABAP with SAP GUI as its UI, which uses a stateful architecture. Then comes the era of RESTful architecture, where you can use RAP/CAP with Fiori UI, and evolution is primarily driven by the push to building cloud-native applications. Now we have arrived in the era of AI-assisted application development, which is fascinating in terms of developer productivity.

Closing: A Call to Arms

We are standing at a transition comparable to the shift from ABAP Dynpro to Fiori, or from on-premise development to cloud-native development. Those who embrace it early will help shape the next generation of SAP applications. Those who wait may eventually find themselves adapting to a world that has already moved on.

reCAP 2026 wasn’t simply another technical conference. It felt like SAP was quietly showing us what the future of enterprise development looks like. As SAP developers, we have a choice: treat AI as another productivity tool or recognize it as the foundation of a fundamentally new way of building applications. I believe that transformation is already underway.

Since returning from reCAP 2026, I’ve started experimenting with AI-first workflows instead of treating AI as an autocomplete tool. Rather than asking Copilot to generate snippets, I’m exploring how MCP-enabled assistants can understand the entire CAP project, reason over documentation, and participate throughout the development lifecycle. That shift in mindset—not the tooling itself—is what convinced me that AI-native development has already begun.

entry.title: Architecture of the Warehouse Task in SAP Logistics Management
entry.content:
Every warehouse operation ultimately comes down to one thing: moving the right stock from the right place to the right destination. In SAP Logistics Management, this physical movement is described by a single document — the Warehouse Task. But we did not simply take the Warehouse Task concept from SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) and carry it over. We did a full redesign: the Warehouse Task in SAP Logistics Management is leaner, structurally simpler, and (together with the SAP Logistics Management stock model)  specifically built to exploit the capabilities of SAP HANA. This post explains the key design decisions and the reasoning behind them.

What Is a Warehouse Task?

A Warehouse Task (WT) is the atomic execution unit for any physical stock movement in the warehouse. It is the system-generated instruction that tells an operator: move this stock from this location to that location. In practice, it is the document surfaced in the SAP Warehouse Logistics mobile app, where operators pick and confirm their work.

A Warehouse Task always describes a physical stock movement. It answers three questions:

  • What: the product, quantity, and optionally the handling unit to be moved
  • From where: the source storage bin and/or source handling unit
  • To where: the destination storage bin and/or destination handling unit

The lifecycle is intentionally simple: a task is either Open, Confirmed, or Canceled. There are no intermediate holding states: When a task exists, it is actionable.

The Principle of One

In EWM, different stock movement operations produce structurally different task documents, each with its own type, fields, and different indicators. 

In SAP Logistics Management, there is one Warehouse Task for all stock movements. The nature of the operation is determined by the reference document category, not the task type. A concrete illustration: there is no HU-WT indicator. A Warehouse Task carries both product fields and handling unit fields. Which ones are populated depends on the operation. 

This has practical benefits today (a uniform API, consistent mobile experience, extensibility by configuration) and matters even more for the future, because a simple, unified model makes it easier to implement new features. The Principle of One is not just a simplification — it is the foundation for consistent feature coverage going forward.

The Process Warehouse Tasks app — HU tasks (such as 8261) and product tasks (such as 8242) in a single unified list.

No Warehouse Order — Just an Optional Task Bundle

In EWM, warehouse tasks are always grouped into a Warehouse Order, the unit of work assigned to an operator. The order is mandatory, and managing its lifecycle adds overhead: orders must be created, assigned, and closed alongside the tasks they contain. In many real-world scenarios the relationship is 1:1,  making the warehouse order a redundant entity that is pure overhead for users.

In SAP Logistics Management, there is no Warehouse Order. Tasks are standalone by default. When grouping is needed, for example to assign a set of tasks to a specific operator or to sequence a multi-step movement, SAP Logistics Management offers a Task Bundle. A bundle covers the main use cases of the EWM warehouse order, but it is optional and lean: tasks work perfectly well without one. This keeps the system simple for straightforward operations while still supporting structured work assignment when the process requires it.

Stock Is Reserved Immediately

A key design principle in SAP Logistics Management is what we call assigned stock: when a warehouse request is created, stock is immediately reserved for it. The reservation is a hard claim and that  particular stock is no longer available for any other purpose.

This is a significant departure from EWM's approach. In EWM, stock availability is tracked as a persistent snapshot at the bin level, stored in table /SCWM/AQUA. Every stock movement triggers updates to this availability snapshot across potentially many bins. In high-throughput warehouses, this leads to a large volume of redundant update operations.

In SAP Logistics Management, we do not persist an availability snapshot. Available stock is computed at read time from the physical stock. This is a design choice that would not have been practical in the era of row-store databases, but on SAP HANA, an aggregation over reservations at query time is fast enough to replace a persisted snapshot entirely. The upside is a much lower write load and significantly reduced risk of inconsistencies, as stock changes generate far fewer downstream updates. The trade-off is that availability checks involve a calculation rather than a simple lookup, but HANA makes that trade-off favorable.

Clear Segregation: Warehouse Tasks vs. Stock Change Documents

In EWM, the warehouse task is a multi-purpose document. It serves as the movement instruction, but it also carries stock-related data and effectively doubles as a stock change record. This is one reason EWM warehouse tasks have a very complex field structure; they fulfill multiple roles in one document.

In SAP Logistics Management, we made a clean architectural separation:

  • The Warehouse Task describes the physical movement of stock. It stays lean, mainly product and HU information, as well as source and destination data. 
  • Stock Change Documents document all kinds of stock changes, such as goods receipts, goods issues, stock type changes, HU creation and deletion, physical inventory differences.

When a Warehouse Task is confirmed, one or more Stock Change Documents are created to record what actually happened. They are linked to the task but are separate documents. This separation matters for several reasons:

  • Not every stock change involves a physical move. A stock type change (for example, moving stock from quality inspection to unrestricted use) generates a Stock Change Document without any physical movement and therefore without a Warehouse Task.
  • Confirmations with exceptions are recorded accurately. If an operator confirms a different quantity or a different handling unit than planned, the Stock Change Document reflects the actual outcome. The task describes the execution; the document is the accounting record.
  • ERP integration and stock reconstruction. Stock Change Documents serve as the anchor for goods movement postings to ERP, and are also used to reconstruct stock in reversal scenarios such as a goods issue reversal.

A Lean Stock Model Built for HANA

The Warehouse Task redesign does not stand alone. It is part of a broader rethinking of the stock model itself.

In EWM, stock information is distributed across 10+ tables in the /LIME/ and /SCWM/ namespaces. 

In SAP Logistics Management, physical stock lives in a single table, and a second table describes all entities that can contain stock (handling units and storage bins) as well as their relationships. This data economy is intentional: fewer tables mean fewer writes, fewer consistency checks, and a simpler mental model for everyone working with the system.

The Manage Stock app — physical stock including information about handling units, storage bins, and reference documents in a single unified view.

This design is made possible by SAP HANA. Aggregating stock across reservations, computing availability, and handling concurrent updates at high throughput are handled efficiently on HANA's columnar engine.  Such operations would have required pre-computed snapshots or denormalized tables on a traditional row-store database. SAP Logistics Management's stock model was designed for HANA from the start.

The result is a system built for data economy and high-throughput, mass-enabled processing from day one — not retrofitted for scale after the fact.

A Redesign, Not a Copy

The SAP Logistics Management Warehouse Task looks familiar if you know EWM, but it is not a copy of the existing concepts. Every aspect described in this post (the single type of task, the optional bundle, the immediate reservation without a persisted availability snapshot, the clean split between task and stock change document) is the result of rethinking the concept from scratch with two goals in mind: make it as simple as possible, and exploit what SAP HANA makes possible. The result is a warehouse execution model that is easier to understand, easier to integrate with, and built to scale.

entry.title: Certified as an SAP Data Architect? Join the practitioner discussion
entry.content:
Congratulations to everyone who has completed or is working toward the new SAP Data Architect certification. Becoming certified is an important milestone. It shows that you are building the knowledge needed to design scalable, governed, AI-ready data ecosystems and connect architecture concepts to business outcomes. 

But certification is also just the beginning. Once you start applying these concepts in real projects, new questions naturally come up: 

  • How do I translate architecture principles into implementation decisions? 
  • How should I approach governance, metadata, ownership, and data quality? 
  • How do I connect SAP Business Data Cloud with existing SAP and non-SAP landscapes? 
  • How do data architecture choices change when AI, advanced analytics, or data products are involved? 
  • What patterns are other practitioners using in the field? 

That is where the Data Professionals group comes in. The Data and Analytics Learning Group is the right place to learn, prepare, and ask questions about learning content. The Data Professionals group is the next step: a practitioner community for discussing real-world architecture, governance, integration, analytics, AI, and SAP Business Data Cloud scenarios with peers, SAP experts, customers, and partners. 

If you have completed the certification, are preparing for it, or are applying the learning journey in your current role, we invite you to join the practitioner discussion here. Bring your project questions, your lessons learned, your architecture challenges, and your practical experience. 

Why don’t you start with one real-world data architecture question you would like to discuss with other practitioners?

entry.title: Candidate privacy settings vs. Candidate search
entry.content:
When applying for a job, a candidate is required to select one of three Search Option Privacy settings.

Make My Profile Visible to:

1) Any company recruiter worldwide. All recruiters can potentially see your profile and approach you for other job opportunities.

2) Any company recruiter in my country only. Only recruiters that are located in the same country that you have chosen as your current country of residence can potentially see your profile and approach you for other job opportunities.

3) Only recruiters managing jobs I apply to. Recruiters can only see your profile when they are responsible for the job you applied to and will not be able to approach you for other job opportunities.

I noticed that when the candidate selects either of the first two options, I am able to find the candidate via  candidate search feature. However, if the candidate selects the third option, it is no longer possible to find them through candidate search.

Is this the expected system behavior, or is it a system issue/bug?

entry.title: changing cost centre after invoice is posted
entry.content:
I wanted to ask an advice on best practice in Public Cloud: 
the current problem we have: if processed invoices are coded to wrong cost centre and corrected by a journal, there is no information on the invoice that shows the journal was recoded, so the same issue can be repeated month on month adding additional workload at month end.

Is there a suggested system solution that can simplify the process of correction - can the cost centre on the original invoice be corrected if the AP is still open? Does it need to go through approval (our approval is based on supplier, not cost centre)?

Curious to know what others are doing in this case.

entry.title: MDG Business Partner Fiori Apps and Technical Catalog SAP_MDG_TC_T Not Available
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

I am working on configuring SAP Master Data Governance (MDG) for Business Partner in SAP S/4HANA 2023 (SP0) and need some guidance regarding the standard Fiori applications and catalogs.

My Requirement

I need to configure two business roles:

1. Requester

  • Create Business Partner Change Requests (CRs)
  • View only their own submitted Change Requests.
  • Track the status of their submitted Change Requests.

2. Approver

  • View pending Change Requests assigned to them.
  • Approve or reject Change Requests.
  • Monitor the status of approved or rejected Change Requests.

Issue

I referred to the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library to identify the standard MDG Business Partner Fiori applications required for these roles. However, I found that the technical catalog SAP_MDG_TC_T, which is associated with several of these applications, is not available in my SAP S/4HANA 2023 SP0 system.

I have attached a screenshot of the applications I identified from the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library for reference.

Questions

  1. Has the technical catalog SAP_MDG_TC_T been replaced, renamed, or deprecated in SAP S/4HANA 2023 SP0?
  2. Which standard Fiori applications should be used to fulfill the following business requirements?
    • Requester: Create Business Partner Change Requests, view only their own requests, and track their status.
    • Approver: View pending Change Requests, approve or reject them, and monitor their status.
  3. Are there alternative Business Catalogs or Technical Catalogs that provide the same functionality in SAP S/4HANA 2023 SP0?
  4. Is there any SAP documentation or SAP Note that explains the replacement or migration of these MDG Fiori catalogs?

Any guidance or recommendations would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you in advance for your support.

entry.title: Re: Change in Update of Activity Consumption in the Quant...
entry.content:
Hello Maruthi,

It is advised to perform at the beginning of the new period and ensure the post closing in previous period was completed. 

And if it is changed to '2', which means the activity update is relevant  to price determination. Therefore, the plan price that is used for the first confirmation in a period is written to the Material Ledger table CKMLLACR and is used for all other confirmations in that period. This price is “frozen” in table CKMLLACR. The plan activity price needs to be stable during one period.

Hope this helps!

entry.title: reCAP 2026 – Recordings of our Annual CAP Developer Conference
entry.content:
On 15 July 2026, the CAP community came together for reCAP 2026 in St. Leon-Rot. Again, tickets were sold out within just a few minutes. We had more than 400 participants onsite and many more joining online.

For everyone who couldn’t attend live, most sessions were recorded. Below is a summary of the recorded sessions, along with links to the recordings. You can find all recordings on YouTube.


 

Opening & Keynote

Intro by Ole Lilienthal
Setting the stage, Ole gave an overview of the Autonomous Enterprise and how CAP plays a key role in enabling the SAP Business AI platform as the underlying platform for the Autonomous Enterprise. He also explained the importance of CAP in the age of AI.  

Recording link

Keynote by Daniel Hutzel
In his keynote, Daniel shared an overview of the biggest CAP achievements since the last reCAP, including AI-related topics.

Recording link

 

Main Track Sessions in Audimax

CAP Tools – What’s New and Hot by Christian Georgi
A tour through the latest and greatest in CAP tooling, including:

  • `cds repl` with queries
  • Debugging apps on Kyma
  • CDS Project outline in Visual Studio Code
  • CAP in browser

Recording link

Optimizing CAP Performance with Data Replication and Caching – Part II by Mike Zaschka
As a follow-up to last year’s talk on cds-caching, this session broadened the view to the full landscape of caching, replication, and federation strategies in the CAP ecosystem. Mike walked through a curated selection of solutions and offered practical guidance on which approach fits which scenario.

Recording link

AI-Assisted Development of CAP Apps by Daniel Schlachter and Stefan Rudi
A look at the CAP MCP development server, which gives AI coding assistants direct access to CAP documentation improving code quality in tools like Claude Code and GitHub Copilot.

Recording link

Expressions and Abstractions – Two Developer Superpowers in CAP by Patrice Bender and DJ Adams
A demonstration of two powerful CAP affordances: expressions (what they are, how to use them, and why they matter) and mocking (letting you iterate fast on data, auth, messaging, and remote services).

Recording link

CAP Plugins: Latest and Greatest by Max Eckert, Dries Van Vaerenbergh, Matthis Vansteenhuyse, and Lisa Julia Nebel
CAP plugins dramatically simplify integration with SAP BTP services. This session demystified the plugin model and spotlighted the enhanced attachments plugin, the renovated change tracking plugin, a new notifications plugin for Java, and the new SAP Build Process Automation plugin.

Recording link

Plug, Connect, Play: CAP Integration Made Effortless by Ram Prasad GS and Bilal El-Massoudi
An introduction to cap-connect, a production-ready, open-source CAP plugin from Aarini Consulting that brings first-class connection management to any CAP application. The talk also showcased how cap-connect powers Prodsphere, Aarini’s own CAP-based product.

Recording link

Production GenAI in CAP: Beyond the Demo by Moudhaffer Azizi
This session explored the architecture behind a production-grade CAP application on SAP BTP that uses RAG to analyse custom ABAP code against Clean Core standards. The session highlighted the journey from a single prompt to a resilient solution orchestrating 14 AI-powered operations.

Recording link

Beyond Vibe Coding: Engineering Deterministic CAP Applications with SDD by Maximilian Hartig and Abdulbasıt Gülşen
A live "0-to-1" walkthrough of Spec-Driven Development using the open-source Spec Kit in Visual Studio Code to transform a raw business requirement into a CAP application.

Recording link

From Click-Ops to Code-Ops (Terraform) by Nicholas Arefta
Nicholas demonstrated how to use Terraform to deploy multitenant apps on SAP BTP. He demonstrated provisioning of a Provider subaccount, deploying a multi-tenant application and onboarding a Subscriber completely hands-free.

Recording link

Custom Business Logic with Predefined Extension Points – Using CDS-Oyster by Nick Josipovic and Konrad Koschel
Together with Subscription Billing and Cloud Foundation, Nick and Konrad built an extensive Extension Box using CAP and the CDS-Oyster Code Sandbox enabling secure code extensions in a complex cloud environment.

Recording link

Implementing Runtime Plugins in CAP Java by Joshua Mitchell
Joshua demonstrated in his live session how to program a basic soft delete plugin for CAP Java using annotations, the IntelliJ debugger, and an expression evaluator.

Recording link

 

Side Track Sessions in W1/W2

Building Code-Based AI Agents with CAP and UI5: From Concept to Production by Wouter Lemaire

Using the Smart Monitoring Agent (previously demonstrated at SAP TechEd) as a real-world example, Wouter showed how to build intelligent, code-based AI agents by combining SAP AI Core with CAP and UI5. The session covered the complete journey from development to deployment.

Recording link

Building AI-enabled Applications with CAP by Max Eckert, Simon Engel, Markus Riedinger, and Peter W. Szabo

An overview of how CAP is evolving to meet the AI era including: exposing CAP services as endpoints for AI agents, leveraging the CAP AI plugin, exploring additional features, and building intelligent, code-driven agents.

Recording link

Building a CAP Application with Latest Best Practices and AI by Lukas Theis, Johannes Vogt, Marc Becker, and Robin de Silva Jayasinghe

A practical, end-to-end walkthrough from idea to production-ready CAP app, combining proven CAP architecture patterns with AI-assisted workflows.

Recording link

Declarative Programming Patterns in CAP - Less Code, More Power by Adrian Görler, Robin de Silva Jayasinghe, and Vitaly Kozyura

A look at how declarative mechanisms in CAP such as calculated elements, annotation-based constraints (e.g. @assert, @assert.range, @assert.format), and field control help to reduce custom code. The session also covered how status transition flows help reducing the need for custom validation logic while ensuring data integrity.

Recording link

Update from the cap-js-community - Experience the latest additions to CAP by Oliver Klemenz

A focused technical update on the latest enhancements in the cap-js-community, with a deep dive into the WebSocket modules and the newly introduced common utilities module.

Recording link

AI-Assisted Security Hardening of CAP Applications by Matthias Braun

Matthias showed how AI-powered tools can help identify authorization gaps, generate comprehensive security tests, and detect attack vectors. This significantly reduces the effort required to build and maintain secure CAP applications.

Recording link

Event Queues Scheduling by Sebastian Van Syckel, Dietrich Mostowoj, Thomas Bonk, and Lars Plessing
A deep dive into Transactional Event Queues, which allow scheduling of events and background tasks for asynchronous, exactly-once processing with strong resilience.

Recording link

Automated Testing with HANA Cloud by Armin Hatting
Armin presented practical approaches to integrating database-supported testing in CAP applications, setting up realistic test environments, and ensuring the functionality of SAP HANA Cloud native logic.

Recording link

Did you know about infix filters and projection functions? by Nico Schoenteich

A live-coding overview of infix filters and projection functions (Node.js) and how they were applied in a recent project to reduce custom service.

Recording link

 

Thank You

A huge thank-you to all speakers, attendees, and community members who made reCAP 2026 such an inspiring day. 🙌 The energy, the ideas, and the conversations are exactly what makes the CAP community special.

If you missed any session, use the recording links above to catch up.

We look forward to seeing how you use what you learned at reCAP 2026. If you have any feedback, please let us know.

Happy coding! 😊

entry.title: Re: Required MBEW - Moving price Time stamp
entry.content:
Hello Venu,

You can check KBA 2335903 - When is TIMESTAMP field updated in table MBEW? - SAP ERP & SAP S/4HANA - SAP for Me

The field MBEW-TIMESTAMP is updated when there are changes in the moving average price in the corresponding valuation table (MBEW, EBEW, QBEW or OBEW) and such changes are performed by goods movements or by invoice postings.

Hope this helps!

entry.title: Re: 🌟 Welcome to the SAP Community Group for Brisbane! 🌟
entry.content:
Hi everyone! 

I'm Faith, a UX designer and developer based in South Africa. I’m currently completing my BSc in Computer Science (graduating next year) and am certified in SAP ABAP Cloud backend development.

SAP really appeals to me because of the harmony between system design and backend logic—I love the challenge of architecting clean data models and business logic that power critical real-world processes, while keeping the end-user experience top of mind.

I've dreamed of relocating to Australia for a long time, and Brisbane looks like an incredible tech hub to grow both personally and professionally, so I'm really excited to have found this community!

A few things I'd love to learn from you all:

Entry Paths: For anyone working as an ABAP / BTP developer in Brisbane—what was your path in, especially when starting out?

Local Landscape: Are there local SAP partners, consultancies, or end-user organizations in Queensland that frequently hire junior/associate ABAP developers?

Relocation Insights: For those who made the move to Australia from abroad for an SAP role, what steps or strategies proved most effective in making that transition?

I’m eager to learn, ask questions, and connect with fellow SAP professionals here. Thanks for having me, and I look forward to chatting!

Best regards,

Faith

entry.title: How are you implementing AI-powered search across SAP business documents?
entry.content:
I'm interested in understanding how organizations are approaching AI-powered search across SAP business documents in production.

Typical examples include questions such as:

  • "Find the certificate linked to this delivery."
  • "Show me all contracts with 30-day payment terms."
  • "Which invoices mention this purchase order?"

I'm curious how people are solving this today.

  • Are you relying on Joule and Document Grounding?
  • Are you building your own RAG pipeline?
  • Are you exposing SAP documents to external LLMs such as ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini through a secure gateway?
  • If using external models, how are you enforcing SAP authorizations so users only retrieve documents they are permitted to access?
  • Are there existing solutions that already solve this well, or are most implementations still custom?

One architecture I've been considering is keeping SAP as the system of record while introducing a gateway that can securely route document queries to different AI providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Azure OpenAI, Gemini, or on-premise models) rather than coupling the solution to a single vendor.

I've attached a simple high-level architecture diagram to illustrate the concept.

I'd be interested to hear what people are actually deploying in production, what has worked well, and any lessons learned.

entry.title: How to enable Email Notifications for SES and PR Approval Workflow
entry.content:
We have identified that standard email notifications are not being sent to business users for the following workflow scenarios:

  • Service Entry Sheet Approval
    • Supplier creates and submits the Service Entry Sheet from SAP Business Network.
    • SES is successfully integrated into SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.
    • The SES is pending approval by the Purchase Requisition Requisitioner (or designated approver).
    • However, no email notification is sent to the requisitioner/approver informing them that an SES is awaiting approval.
  • Purchase Requisition Approval
    • A Purchase Requisition is submitted into the Flexible Workflow.
    • The assigned approver does not receive an email notification indicating that a PR is awaiting approval.

Currently,Users must manually log into the system or check My Inbox to identify pending approvals.

We would like to understand whether this is:

  • Standard system behavior,
  • A configuration that needs to be enabled,
  • Or a current product limitation.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Mastering SAP Datasphere Replication Scheduling
entry.content:
Data integration is the heartbeat of any modern data warehouse. In SAP Datasphere, keeping data accurate, timely, and reliable depends largely on how effectively data flows, replication flows, and task chains are automated.

To support a variety of business and technical requirements, SAP Datasphere provides three scheduling approaches that work together:

  1. Frequency-Based
  2. Simple Schedule
  3. Cron Expression

Understanding when to use each method can help organizations simplify operations, improve maintainability, and ensure data is available when the business needs it.

Automated scheduling eliminates manual intervention and ensures data is refreshed consistently. Whether you're loading transactional data every hour, refreshing master data daily, or running complex month-end processes, the right scheduling strategy can:

  • Improve data freshness and availability
  • Reduce operational effort
  • Increase reliability of reporting and analytics
  • Optimize system resource utilization
  • Simplify administration and maintenance

The key is selecting a scheduling approach that matches your business requirements without adding unnecessary complexity.

Frequency-Based Scheduling:

           Frequency-Based Scheduling is the standard method used by most SAP Datasphere users. Instead of creating custom schedules, users simply select predefined execution intervals from the available options.

SAP Datasphere supports:

  • Every 5, 10, 15, 20, or 30 minutes
  • Hourly
  • Daily
  • Weekly
  • Monthly

Benefits

  • Easy to configure No technical expertise is required.

Common Use Cases

  • Hourly Transaction Processing - Load sales orders, inventory transactions, or operational metrics every hour.
  • Daily Master Data Refreshes - Update customer, product, and organizational data during off-peak hours.
  • Weekly Synchronization - Refresh business partner, supplier, or vendor information on a weekly basis.
  • Monthly Data Snapshots - Create period-end financial and operational snapshots for reporting purposes.

Simple Schedule: The Business-Friendly Scheduling Engine

           While Frequency-Based Scheduling defines when jobs run, the Simple Schedule is the SAP Datasphere interface that manages and executes those schedules. Think of it as the user-friendly layer that converts scheduling selections into automated background jobs. Users can configure schedules through a graphical interface without needing to understand technical scheduling syntax.

Key Advantages

  • No Technical Knowledge Required - Business users can create schedules without learning cron expressions or scripting languages.
  • Daylight Saving Time Awareness - Schedules automatically adapt based on the selected regional time zone, helping organizations manage daylight-saving changes.
  • Background Automation - Once configured, jobs execute automatically without ongoing intervention.
  • Easy Maintenance - Schedules are simple to understand and can be easily managed by administrators or business users.

Typical Use Cases

  • Daily persistence jobs at 2:00 AM
  • Weekly replication every Monday and Friday
  • Monthly warehouse refreshes on the first day of the month
  • Standard ETL and ELT workloads
  • For many organizations, Simple Schedule becomes the default scheduling approach because it balances flexibility with ease of use.

Cron Expression Scheduling: Advanced Control and Precision

             Sometimes business requirements go beyond predefined schedules. Perhaps a process must run every 15 minutes during business hours only. Or maybe month-end processing needs to run on the last Sunday of every month. For these advanced scenarios, SAP Datasphere provides Cron Expression Scheduling. Cron expressions allow users to define highly customized execution patterns using a structured scheduling syntax.

Cron scheduling supports:

  • Specific weekdays
  • Multiple executions per day
  • Business-hour-only processing
  • Date range restrictions
  • Month-end scheduling
  • Advanced recurring patterns

Advanced Business Scenarios

  • Business-Hour Processing - Run data loads every 15 minutes during office hours while reducing cloud resource consumption outside business periods.
  • Month-End Refreshes - Execute data warehouse refreshes on the last Sunday of every month.
  • Multiple Daily Executions - Run critical financial updates at: 03:00, 09:00, 15:00
  • This level of flexibility is difficult or impossible to achieve through standard scheduling options alone.

Choosing the Right Scheduling Option :

Business Requirement
Recommended Option
Every few minutes or hoursFrequency-Based Scheduling
Daily, weekly, or monthly loadsSimple Schedule
Business-friendly configurationSimple Schedule
Standard ETLSimple Schedule
Multiple custom run times per dayCron Expression
Complex calendar-based logicCron Expression
Last weekday or last Sunday schedulingCron Expression
Advanced enterprise scheduling needsCron Expression

entry.title: Best Practice for SAP Business One Private Cloud to Azure DR Architecture
entry.content:
Dear SAP Experts Team,

I require your guidance and recommendations for implementing a Disaster Recovery (DR) architecture for SAP Business One on Microsoft SQL Server hosted in a Private Cloud, with Microsoft Azure as the DR site.

Current Environment

ComponentConfiguration

SAP Business OneMicrosoft SQL Server
Production ArchitecturePrivate Cloud
SQL ServerDedicated Database Server
SAP SLDInstalled on a separate Server
SAP Business One ClientInstalled on a separate RDP/Terminal Server
DR TargetMicrosoft Azure

Current Concerns

AreaConcern
Azure Site Recovery (ASR)VM replication works, but during failover Azure needs time to spin up the VM, increasing RTO.
SAP SLDAfter failover, the Azure VM receives a new hardware key, causing SAP License/SLD issues.
Dedicated Azure SLD ServerKeeping a dedicated Azure VM for SLD increases the overall DR cost.
SQL Server ReplicationAsynchronous replication may result in minor data loss (RPO) during an unexpected disaster.
CostLooking for a cost-effective DR solution without maintaining multiple always-running Azure VMs.

Questions

  1. What is the SAP-recommended architecture for SAP Business One Private Cloud DR on Microsoft Azure?
  2. What is the best practice for SAP SLD and License Server in a DR environment?
  3. Is there any supported method to automatically switch from the Production SLD to the Azure SLD during a disaster?
  4. Is Azure Site Recovery (ASR) alone sufficient, or should SQL Server technologies such as Always On Availability Groups, Log Shipping, or Failover Cluster Instances be considered instead?
  5. How can the RTO and RPO be minimized while keeping Azure infrastructure costs low?
  6. Has anyone implemented a similar SAP Business One Private Cloud to Azure DR architecture with automatic failover and minimal downtime?

Any recommendations, architecture diagrams, SAP Notes, or real-world implementation experiences would be greatly appreciated.

entry.title: Re: Equivalence number not copied to Proces order from Ma...
entry.content:
Hello,

You can check KBA 2566647 - S4/HANA: How the price differences are distributed in a joint production scenario - SAP for Me

Please maintain output materials as a receiver and make sure the percentage as 100

Remark: By design, Actual Costing is not reading the settlement rule from orders to define the apportionment. In Actual Costing, orders are linked to production processes. The movements of all orders linked to the same production process are recorded under the same Actual BOM. This explains why it makes no sense using the settlement rule of one single order for the apportionment.

entry.title: [Public Sector] SAP Learning Curriculum for Public Sector
entry.content:
SAP Public Sector Learning Content Overview

SAP offers a comprehensive set of learning resources for SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition and Private Cloud Edition for Public Sector, targeting state, local, education, and non-profit customers who need to manage public sector-specific budget execution and reporting requirements.

The learning paths in this curriculum have two different learning formats: self-paced learning assets and classroom & instructor-led courses.

The classroom trainings are deeper and more detailed, and the participants receive access to a training system in which they can practice their newly acquired knowledge and skills.

Public Sector, Private Cloud Edition

Course Code & Title

Topics Covered

Direct Link to the Learning Asset

IPS810 – SAP Grants Management – Grantee

Master data (grantor, grant, expenditure/revenue types, grantor program, fund); Grant lifecycle (project idea to final report); Budgeting and availability control; Revenue to increase budget; Integration with CO, FM, HR, AA, MM, and SD; Billing and calculation of grants from sponsor; Grants in foreign currency; Cost sharing between recipient and grantor; Overhead; Disclosure of sponsored funds; Electronic file management; Internal and external reporting

3-days classroom training: https://training.sap.com/course/IPS810

IPS910 – Funds Management: Processes, Organization and Configuration

Master Data (Funds Center, Commitment Item, Fund, Functional Area, Funded Program); Budget Planning; Budget Execution; Budget Control System (BCS) including budget types, versions, transfers; Availability Control (AVC); Integration with FI, CO, MM, HR, PS, SD; Cash Desk; Year-End Closing; Reporting

3-days classroom training: https://training.sap.com/course/IPS910

IPS510 – SAP Public Sector Collections and Disbursements (PSCD) – Financial Contract Accounting for Public Sector

Contract account management (FI-CA); SEPA payment run; Flexible dunning; Enhanced incoming correspondence; Integration with SAP S/4HANA; Tax and Revenue Management; SAP Fee Solution; Social Services; Collections Management; Convergent Invoicing; Multi Channel Foundation; Joint and several liability

2-days classroom training: https://training.sap.com/course/IPS510

IPS500 – SAP Tax and Revenue Management (SAP TRM) – End-to-End Processes Overview

End-to-end overview of Tax and Revenue Management processes for public sector

Free Learning Journey: https://learning.sap.com/courses/discovering-e2e-processes-of-sap-tax-and-revenue-management-for-public-sector?searchId=126a1f45-3d4c-4a32-b281-7897466bfba3&listPosition=1

1-day classroom training: https://training.sap.com/course/IPS500

IPS520 – Core Tax Administration Processes in SAP Tax and Revenue Management

Core tax administration processes within SAP TRM

Free learning journey: https://learning.sap.com/courses/exploring-core-tax-administration-processes-in-sap-tax-and-revenue-management?searchId=e7433894-1da5-4f1b-a0bb-89cbe4d79a93&listPosition=1

 

5-days classroom training: https://training.sap.com/course/IPS520

IPS610 – SAP for Public Sector – Social Service Management

Social Service Management processes for public sector organizations

3-days classroom training: https://training.sap.com/course/IPS610

IPS710 – SAP Grants Management – Grantor

Grants Management from the grantor perspective

Course is now in re-design

 

 

Public Sector, Public Cloud Edition

 Free Digital Learning Journeys (on Learning.SAP.com)

These three core learning journeys are freely available and self-paced, offering over 400 pages of educational content that is continuously expanded:

 

Course Code & Title

Direct Link to the Learning Asset

IPSC1

Implementing Budget Management Process in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition for Public Sector

7 Units, ~9 hours

Free learning journey: https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-budget-management-in-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-public-sector-higher-education?searchId=1a838dbe-7985-4d30-a529-a2186aecdc1c&listPosition=1

Implementing Grants Management Process in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition for Public Sector

5 Unit, ~3 hours

Free learning journey: https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-grants-management-in-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-public-sector-higher-education?searchId=91104703-d052-4c24-b95e-14012dcf516f&listPosition=1

entry.title: Groovy in your SAP Cloud Integration iFlows is application code. Here is what to check.
entry.content:
As PI/PO approaches the end of mainstream maintenance in 2027, most of us are moving
integration onto SAP Integration Suite / Cloud Integration. In the process, a lot of
custom logic ends up in Groovy script steps. And a Groovy script step is not a mapping
expression: it is full application code running on the tenant. It can run OS commands,
open sockets, read the filesystem and handle credentials.

That makes iFlow scripts a real security surface, and one that most SAP security reviews
still skip. Below are the patterns worth checking in your own script steps, with small
examples.

1. OS command execution from message data

A classic mistake is building a shell command out of a header or payload field:

def name = message.getHeaders().get("X-Document-Name")
def out = ["/bin/sh", "-c", "process " + name].execute().text

If `X-Document-Name` is attacker controlled, so is the command. Avoid `.execute()`,
`Runtime.exec` and `ProcessBuilder` on anything derived from the message. If you truly
need a process call, pass a fixed argument array and validate every value against an
allow list.

2. XXE in XML parsing

`XmlSlurper` and `XmlParser` resolve DTDs and external entities by default:

def xml = new XmlSlurper().parse(
new ByteArrayInputStream(message.getBody(byte[].class)))

A crafted payload can read local files or reach internal endpoints. Disable DTDs and
external entities explicitly:

def parser = new XmlSlurper()
parser.setFeature("http://apache.org/xml/features/disallow-doctype-decl", true)
parser.setFeature("http://xml.org/sax/features/external-general-entities", false)

3. Path traversal on file names

def name = message.getHeaders().get("X-Document-Name")
new File("/tmp/inbound/" + name).text = body

A value like `../../somewhere` breaks out of the intended folder. Normalize the path and
confirm it still sits under the base directory before writing.

4. Hardcoded secrets and disabled TLS checks

Credentials pasted into a script step, or a client that turns off certificate or hostname
verification to "make it work", both survive far longer than anyone intends. Use the
Security Material store for secrets, and never disable TLS verification in production
scripts.

5. Sensitive data in the message log

messageLog?.setStringProperty("payload", message.getBody(String.class))

Convenient for debugging, but full payloads can carry PII, tokens or bank data straight
into logs that a wider group can read. Log identifiers, not bodies.

Why this matters

None of this is exotic. It is the same class of issue we already scan for in custom ABAP,
just moved to a new runtime and a new language. The problem is that it is spread across
many iFlows and rarely reviewed line by line.

We built a scanner for exactly this at RedRays. It connects to your Integration Suite
runtime, pulls the iFlow content and statically analyzes each Groovy, JavaScript and XSLT
script step for the issues above, returning a CVSS score and the exact file and line, in
the same console as our ABAP scans.

> **Disclosure:** I work at RedRays, so treat this paragraph as vendor context, not a
> neutral recommendation. The checklist above stands on its own and you can apply it by
> hand or with any tool you prefer.

If you have run into other risky Groovy patterns in CPI, I would like to hear them in the
comments so we can build a fuller list for the community.

entry.title: Re: Internal & External Procurement in asset management
entry.content:
Greetings,

immediate purchase requisition if stock is unavailable is not possible in standard. As you observed yourself you need to run MRP to generate procurement proposals. I guess you could develop some enhancements to run MRP after order is saved but this purchase requisition would not be linked to the order. You would need to be careful here to limit the MRP run only to specific materials.

The mandatory fields when selecting item category N can be defaulted. This is useful because usually employees working with maintenance order to not know or do not need to know the details. The following details can be defaulted:

  • Cost Element
  • Purchasing Organization
  • Purchasing Group
  • Material Group

Create the profile:

SPRO -> Plant Maintenance and Customer Service -> Maintenance and Service Processing -> Maintenance and Service Orders -> Functions and Settings for Order Types -> Procurement -> Create Default Value Profiles for External Procurement

Assign profile to maintenance order/plant combination:

SPRO -> Plant Maintenance and Customer Service -> Maintenance and Service Processing -> Maintenance and Service Orders -> Functions and Settings for Order Types -> Default Values for Task List Data and Profile Assignments

Best regards, M.

entry.title: Re: Groovy in your SAP Cloud Integration iFlows is application code. Here is what to check.
entry.content:
This is a very timely and practical post. Groovy scripting in SAP Cloud Integration is often misunderstood as just a ‘developer’s add‑on,’ but your explanation makes it clear that it is application code that directly shapes integration logic. The examples you’ve shared highlight how Groovy can extend iFlow capabilities beyond configuration, enabling dynamic payload handling, custom validations, and enriched transformations.

From an enterprise architecture perspective, the key takeaway is that Groovy should be treated with the same governance and lifecycle discipline as any other application code — versioning, testing, and documentation are critical. This ensures that integrations remain maintainable and compliant as they scale.

entry.title: Re: How are you implementing AI-powered search across SAP business documents?
entry.content:
In production landscapes, we’ve seen three main approaches to AI‑powered search across SAP business documents:

  • Joule + Document Grounding: Joule provides natural language understanding while Document Grounding ensures queries are resolved against SAP’s system of record. This is the most ‘native’ approach and keeps authorizations intact.

  • Custom RAG Pipelines: Some teams build retrieval‑augmented generation pipelines where SAP documents are indexed in a vector store (e.g., HANA Cloud or Datasphere embeddings). Queries are enriched with metadata like company code, contract type, or payment terms before being passed to an LLM.

  • Secure Gateway to External LLMs: Others expose SAP documents to external models (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Azure OpenAI) via a controlled gateway. The critical piece here is enforcing SAP authorizations — typically by filtering search results at the gateway before they reach the LLM.

Key lessons learned:

  • Always treat SAP as the system of record; AI should augment search, not bypass governance.

  • Authorization enforcement is non‑negotiable — whether you use Joule or external models, users must only see documents they are entitled to.

  • Metadata enrichment (e.g., payment terms, partner IDs, document type) dramatically improves search accuracy.

  • Keep the architecture modular so you can swap AI providers without re‑engineering the SAP integration.

entry.title: Connecting SAP PaPM Cloud (Universal Model) to Google BigQuery via SAP HANA Cloud Virtual Tables.
entry.content:
Architecture Overview

The data flow follows a clean 3-tier sequence:

  • Google BigQuery: Holds the raw transactional data (e.g., ticket extracts, gross amounts, payment methods).
  • SAP HANA Cloud (SDA / Remote Source): Connects to BigQuery via the bigqueryrest adapter and exposes the tables as Virtual Tables inside a designated schema (e.g., SAP_PAPM_ADMIN).
  • SAP PaPM Cloud (Universal Model): Consumes the Virtual Table through a HANA Schema connection to map fields into the modeling environment.

Key Setup Steps & Lessons Learned

1. SSL Certificates in SAP HANA Cloud

Before establishing the BigQuery Remote Source, ensure that the required Google Trust Services certificates are imported into the SAP HANA Cloud Certificate Store and properly bound to the SDA PSE.

Tip: If you encounter SSL handshake issues during connection tests, verify that the GTS Root R4 certificate is properly imported and bound to _SAP_DB_ACCESS_PSE_SDA.

2. Creating the Remote Source and Virtual Table

Using SAP HANA Database Explorer, we configured the Remote Source pointing to the BigQuery project and dataset. Then, we created the target Virtual Table inside the SAP_PAPM_ADMIN schema.

Figure 1 - BigQuery Remote Source, Virtual Table setup in SAP HANA Explorer, and HANA Schema connection configuration in PaPM Cloud.

3. Handling the PaPM Cloud UI Metadata Validation (^.{0,200}$)

When synchronizing the HANA Schema connection in PaPM Cloud, you might encounter a string length format validation error similar to:

Value 'Specifies whether the cached is enabled for the view or not: ...'
is not in specified format '^.{0,200}$'
Column: Description

 

Why does this happen? When HANA Cloud creates a Virtual Table over BigQuery, it inherits extended metadata comments into the table/view description. PaPM Cloud's UI enforces a strict 200-character limit on description fields during model synchronization.

The Solutions

We implemented two complementary solutions to bypass and fix this issue completely:

  • Option A: Apply a Model Filter in PaPM Connection (Recommended): In the General Information tab of your PaPM HANA Schema connection, use the Model Filter field to specify only your target table (e.g., XXXXXXX_extractos_transacciones). This automatically excludes system views or unrelated schema objects with long descriptions.

Figure 2 - Using the Model Filter setting in PaPM Cloud Connection to isolate the target Virtual Table and avoid metadata issues.

  • Option B: Overwrite the HANA Table Comment via SQL: Run a short SQL statement in SAP HANA Database Explorer to replace the inherited metadata comment with a concise string:

COMMENT ON TABLE "SAP_PAPM_ADMIN"."IXXXXXXX_extractos_transacciones"
IS 'Transacciones BigQuery';

Figure 3 - Overwriting the inherited metadata comment using SQL in SAP HANA Database Explorer.

Result: Consuming Data in PaPM Universal Model

Once the description comment was updated in HANA and the Model Filter was applied in PaPM Cloud, the model synchronization completed seamlessly. We mapped the BigQuery fields to our PaPM Environment dimensions, and running a "Show Data" action immediately displayed live BigQuery records inside the SAP PaPM Cloud UI!

Figure 4 - Virtual Table functions and field mappings active inside the PaPM Universal Model environment.

Figure 5 - Live BigQuery records successfully displayed using the Show Data action inside SAP PaPM Cloud UI.

Bonus: Direct BigQuery SQL Query

For verification, you can also query the source dataset directly in Google BigQuery:

SELECT *
FROM `gen-lang-client-003xfdsf.xxxxxxxx_es_jesap.extractos_transacciones`
LIMIT 100;

Figure 6 - Source transactional dataset and SQL query execution in Google BigQuery console.

Conclusion

Connecting BigQuery to SAP PaPM Cloud via HANA Virtual Tables provides a powerful, real-time data flow without double-storing data. The two key takeaways from our experience:

  • Always ensure SSL certificates (GTS Root R4) are properly aligned in SAP HANA Cloud Certificate Store.
  • Keep table descriptions concise and leverage the Model Filter in PaPM Cloud connections to prevent UI metadata validation errors.

Have you tried connecting external cloud databases to SAP PaPM Cloud? Share your thoughts and questions in the comments below!
